title: Professions Overview
subtitle: What you are in the economy

content.text: Paperwork outlives people. It was never yours.
Skills are what you can do. A Profession is what you are in the economy.

The lane law: a skill makes you able; a profession makes it pay. Skills are competence — anywhere, for anyone. A profession is position: a client list, a license, a name. Professions bleed into skills (prerequisites, contextual advantage) but never grant skill dice (skill dice live on The Twelve Skills).

content.label: THE TATTOO-ARTIST LAW
content.text: A profession is fully portable. The name travels; premises don't exist. Walls belong to the squad's base, never to your trade (see The Base).
Profession Points
Profession Points (PP) are earned by doing the job, not by rolling for it. There are exactly two sources — the job levels the job:
+2 PP per downtime period spent on your trade's Work verb — which also pays credits (income, below).
+1 PP when you exercise your trade's field verb on a mission, max 1 per mission. This is how Carvers, Wranglers, and Guides level — their workplace is the mission.
Perks are bought with PP — 4 to 8 PP each; a few also cost credits, because some kit is money and expertise. Each profession's page lists its perks, costs, and verbs.
Tiers — Sideline, Established, Name
Tiers unlock by perks owned, starting from the entry perk.

headers: What it means in the world
rows: You do this for extra money
You have regulars and a reputation
The district knows. So do the corps.

content.label: ONE NAME
content.text: Take as many Sidelines as your calendar survives, but T3 in one profession only — the world knows you as one thing.
Income
The Work verb pays per downtime work period, by rank — always beating the generic side job (100–200 cr, no PP), never beating the calendar.

content.label: THE BALANCE LAW
content.text: Every downtime period spent working your trade is a period not spent training for combat. Stats, skills, and Profession Points all eat the same scarce calendar.
Capstoning a profession runs ≈ 30–40 PP — about 15–20 dedicated downtime periods, the same order as training a stat from 4 to 10. Becoming a Name costs what peak human costs (see The Anchor Table).
Obligations

Several Established and Name perks carry an Obligation line — a standing expectation that comes with the perk and is part of its price. The cost of being known is being known. Each Obligation is printed with its perk.

Registry is a privilege. Licensed practitioners enjoy catalog access, dispute arbitration, and quarterly audit. Unregistered practitioners enjoy neither protection nor records, and are reminded that absence from the database is itself a data point.
Everything dies with the person
Nothing transfers. Rank, perks, clients, and banked PP die with the character. The license was in their name — paperwork outlives people, and it was never yours.
The launch seven

rows: repair & mods — the shell clock's best friend
scar treatment & detox — the long bill
yield & markets — what the kill is worth

content.text: The lane split, printed: the Fixer owns who; the Guide owns where. Baseline verbs live in skills — anyone can drive a truck on a Tech check, calm a hound on Awareness, field-dress a kill on Harvesting. Ownership and scale live in professions: the Rigger has the truck; the Wrangler's hound stays trained; the Carver's cuts sell.
Profession as build choice
Three augment trees run on supply lines, and your trade decides which one feeds you:
Systems: Tinkerer gear or Fixer intel (see Systems).
Precision: Tinkerer handloads or Fixer load-knowledge (see Precision).
Influence: Wrangler raise or Fixer buy (see Influence).
